text:
The world is facing multiple water challenges from contamination of water resources to destruction of critical wetlands, over-extraction of water, severe flooding, and unequal access to clean water and sanitation. Investing in better management of waters has been undermined by poor implementation, inadequate financing and incorrect identification of water problems and issues. This has led to the over-use of concrete solutions and inflexible infrastructure, rather than looking at the flexibility that can be brought to water use and management through demand management and other approaches, including water sharing.

Our article proposes a change in perspective through the “Beyond Growth” lens. This requires moving beyond the conventional growth-based paradigm and viewing water resources from a systems perspective that is founded on principles of reciprocity and justice. Focusing on the needs of the most vulnerable and aligning progress towards human well-being with environmental sustainability can help ensure that real transformation of water resources is possible beyond the grip of rent seeking and path dependence.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
